God takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked — Paul's 'what if God, choosing to show his wrath, bore with great patience' suggests a restrained divine judgment; Ezekiel's affirmation that God does not desire destruction provides the OT counterbalance that prevents reading Paul's vessel-of-wrath language as divine sadism.

23.note: The riches of God's glory revealed to those he chose — the conjunction of divine glory and mercy that Paul describes in the vessels of mercy is anticipated in Isaiah's portrait of God's sovereign creative purpose (to display his own glory) fulfilled in the redemption of his people.

24.note: The restoration of David's fallen tent 'so that they may possess the remnant of Edom and all the nations' — Paul's 'called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles' fulfills the Amos promise that the reconstituted Davidic kingdom would incorporate the Gentile nations, cited in Acts 15:16-17 as the Jamesian interpretation of the same fulfillment.

25.note: 'I will call them my people who are not my people; and I will call her my loved one who is not my loved one' — Hosea's oracle promising the restoration of unfaithful Israel, applied by Paul to the Gentiles. The movement from not-my-people to my-people in Hosea, which addressed a restored Israel, is read by Paul as anticipating precisely the inclusion of Gentile non-people into God's people.
I will call them my people who are not my people

26.note: 'In the very place where it was said to them, You are not my people, there they will be called children of the living God' — Hosea's reversal of the Lo-Ammi oracle: those declared not-my-people become children of God. Paul applies this to Gentiles, reading Hosea's promise of Israel's restoration as anticipating a more expansive inclusion than Hosea named.
Children of the living God — Hosea applied to Gentiles

27.note: 'Though the number of the Israelites be like the sand by the sea, only the remnant will be saved, for the Lord will carry out his sentence on the earth with speed and finality' — Isaiah's remnant theology: the sheer numerical size of Israel does not guarantee corporate salvation; God's judgment falls within Israel, with only a remnant preserved. Paul uses this to show that present Jewish unbelief is consistent with Scripture's own pattern.
Though Israel's number be like the sand — a remnant saved

28.note: The Lord will carry out his sentence on the earth with speed and finality — the divine judgment embedded in the remnant oracle confirms that God's word has not failed; it has been executed precisely as Isaiah predicted, in the separation of the remnant from the rest.

29.note: 'Unless the LORD Almighty had left us some survivors, we would have become like Sodom, we would have been like Gomorrah' — Isaiah's opening chapter, which already imagines Israel's near-total destruction, provides Paul his proof that remnant preservation has always been an act of divine mercy, not national entitlement. The seed left by the LORD is the grace within judgment.
Unless the Lord had left us descendants

30.note: Those who pursue righteousness and who seek the LORD — Paul's paradox that Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness obtained it while Israel who pursued it did not traces the surprising reversal that Isaiah already anticipated: the mission to the Gentiles would produce fruit where Israel's own religious effort had stalled.

31.note: Moses declaring that Israel's righteousness would consist in keeping all this law — Paul's description of Israel 'pursuing the law as the way of righteousness' without obtaining it is the failure of the Deuteronomic program; the law could define righteousness but not produce it in sinful flesh.

32.note: The cornerstone laid in Zion as the test of Israel's response — Paul's 'they pursued it not by faith but as if it were by works; they stumbled over the stumbling stone' introduces the Isaiah 28:16 / Isaiah 8:14 complex (quoted in v33) as the explanation of Israel's stumbling: the stone-laying in Zion (the Messiah) was the test that exposed the basis of their pursuit.

33.note: 'See, I lay a stone in Zion, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone for a sure foundation; the one who relies on it will never be stricken with panic' — Paul combines this with Isaiah 8:14 to form a composite stone-text. The LXX of Isaiah 28:16 adds 'in him' (ep' autō), turning the promise explicitly personal; Paul reads this as the Messiah, belief in whom brings no shame.

'He will be a holy place; for both Israel and Judah he will be a stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall' — the second stone-text Paul combines with Isaiah 28:16. The same Zion reality that is foundation for the believing becomes the stumbling-stone for those who pursue it by works; Israel stumbled over the Christ whom the two texts together describe.
A stone that causes stumbling — to Israel

1.note: Paul's 'heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel that they may be saved' echoes Moses's intercession after the golden calf: 'But if not, blot me out of the book you have written.' Both Paul (Romans 9:3) and Moses stand as covenant mediators interceding for a people who have broken faith; Paul frames his apostolic passion within this Mosaic intercessory pattern.

The Servant interceding for transgressors — Paul's 'my heart's desire and prayer to God for the Israelites is that they may be saved' places Paul in the position of the Servant who intercedes for those he loves even when they do not receive him.

2.note: Phinehas's 'zeal' (Heb. qin'ah) for God halted the plague at Baal-Peor — the paradigmatic OT instance of zeal for God that was also, in that case, rightly directed. Paul acknowledges Israel's zeal is genuine (as Phinehas's was) but notes that Israel's zeal lacks epignōsis; the problem is not the energy but the object and means.

'Zeal for your house consumes me' — the psalmist's passionate devotion to God's honor (applied to Christ in John 2:17) is the register Paul uses for Israel's zeal; the same word (zēlos) covers both the psalmist's spirit and Israel's law-keeping drive, but without knowledge the passion cannot find its proper end.

Phinehas whose zeal for God was not accompanied by understanding — Paul's 'zeal not based on knowledge' echoes the prophetic critique of misguided religious intensity; Israel's zeal for the law is real but misdirected, like the OT examples of sincere but ill-directed devotion.

3.note: All our righteous acts are like filthy rags — Israel's attempt to 'establish their own' righteousness rather than submitting to God's righteousness stands in the tradition of Isaiah's confession about self-generated holiness that cannot satisfy the divine standard; Paul now specifies that God's righteousness has arrived in Christ.

4.note: 'It pleased the LORD for the sake of his righteousness to magnify his law and make it glorious' — the law's glory was always instrumental to God's righteousness; Paul's claim that Christ is the telos (culmination/end) of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes is the fulfillment of what the law was always pointing toward, not its abolition.

The new covenant replacing the old — Paul's 'Christ is the culmination (telos) of the law so that there may be righteousness for everyone who believes' articulates the telos toward which the Mosaic administration was always pointing; Jeremiah's new covenant (Torah internalized by the Spirit) is the trajectory Christ fulfills and surpasses.

5.note: 'Keep my decrees and laws, for the person who obeys them will live by them' — Moses's summary of Torah obedience: life through doing. Paul cites this as the standard of law-righteousness to contrast with faith-righteousness; Leviticus 18:5 defines the terms of the law's own promise, which the law could not ultimately deliver given the weakness of the flesh.
The man who does these things will live

6.note: 'It is not up in heaven... Nor is it beyond the sea... No, the word is very near you; it is in your mouth and in your heart so you may obey it' — Moses about the accessibility of the Torah. Paul re-reads this christologically: 'Who will ascend into heaven?' means bringing Christ down (the incarnation), 'who will descend into the deep?' means raising him from the dead (the resurrection). The word's nearness is now the word of faith concerning Christ, removing any excuse of inaccessibility.

Moses's 'who will ascend to heaven to get it and proclaim it to us?' — Paul rereads Deuteronomy 30:12 Christologically: the 'ascending to heaven' is glossed as bringing Christ down (questioning the necessity of the incarnation) and 'descending into the deep' as bringing Christ up from the dead (questioning the necessity of the resurrection); the near word of Deut 30 is the gospel, already accomplished.
The word is near you — righteousness by faith

7.note: Moses's 'who will cross the sea to get it?' — Paul glosses the descent into the sea as descent into the abyss (Hades) and return (the resurrection); the radical accessibility of the word in Deuteronomy 30 — no heroic journey required — becomes Paul's argument that no additional redemptive labor is needed beyond what Christ has already accomplished.

8.note: The word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart — Paul identifies this near word as 'the message of faith we are proclaiming'; Moses's claim that the covenant word is radically accessible is fulfilled in the gospel proclamation, which requires only hearing and believing rather than heroic ascent or descent.

9.note: 'Before me every knee will bow; by me every tongue will swear' — LXX: 'every tongue will confess to God.' Paul's 'confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord' invokes the divine-name declaration of Isa 45, which reserves this confession for the LORD alone. Calling Jesus 'Lord' in Paul's frame appropriates the Isaianic title; the full citation appears at Romans 14:11 and Philippians 2:10-11.

'And everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved' — the confession of Jesus as Lord and belief in the resurrection leads to the Joel promise Paul quotes explicitly in v13; v9's 'you will be saved' anticipates and grounds the v13 citation, making Joel's universal offer the content of the confession Paul here describes.

The LORD's declaration 'Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool' — Paul's 'declare with your mouth Jesus is Lord' is the confession that the one enthroned at God's right hand (Psalms 110:1) is Jesus; the confession of Jesus as 'Lord' (kyrios) applies the OT divine name and enthronement to the risen Christ.

10.note: Love the LORD with all your heart — Paul's 'with your heart that you believe and are justified, with your mouth that you profess and are saved' reframes the Shema's heart-and-mouth devotion to Yahweh around the saving confession of Christ; the whole-person commitment of Deuteronomy 6 now has its ultimate object in the Lord Jesus.

11.note: 'Anyone who believes in him will never be put to shame' — the LXX reading of the Zion cornerstone text, already cited at Romans 9:33, here provides the scriptural guarantee of the faith-confession described in v9-10. Paul returns to this verse as the closing scriptural warrant for belief, forming an inclusio with its earlier use.
Anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame

12.note: God's call to the ends of the earth — 'Turn to me and be saved, all you ends of the earth, for I am God, and there is no other' — Paul's 'the same Lord is Lord of all and richly blesses all who call on him' is the actualization of Isaiah 45:22's universal invitation through the confession of Christ as Lord.

13.note: 'And everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved' — Joel's promise in the context of the outpouring of the Spirit (Joel 2:28-32, cited by Peter at Pentecost in Acts 2:21) is here cited by Paul as the universal scope of salvation: 'everyone' without ethnic qualification, Jew and Gentile alike, on the same terms of calling on the Lord's name.
Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved

14.note: Proclaim his salvation, declare his glory among the nations — the chain of calling → believing → hearing → preaching → sending that Paul constructs in vv14-15 inhabits the Psalter's vision of the divine name proclaimed to all nations; the missionary sequence Paul describes is the form the psalmist's call to proclamation takes in the new covenant era.

15.note: 'How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news, who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion: Your God reigns!' — the herald announcing Israel's return from Babylonian exile becomes for Paul the type of the apostolic proclamation of the gospel. The same movement — herald sent, news announced, hearing, responding — maps from Isaiah's exodus-return to the proclamation of Christ.
How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news
How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news

16.note: 'Lord, who has believed our message? And to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?' — the Servant Song's opening lament that the message was not believed is Paul's proof that Israel's unbelief in the face of proclamation was itself anticipated in Scripture. Isaianic proclamation was met with the same unbelief that now greets the gospel; the pattern is not new.
Lord, who has believed our report?

17.note: 'The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you... You must listen to him' — the Mosaic provision of the authoritative prophet whose word must be heeded is the background for 'the word of Christ'; faith comes from hearing, and the word to be heard is Christ's own prophetic proclamation, the culmination of the Mosaic prophetic line.

God's word going out and not returning empty but accomplishing what he desires — Paul's 'faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word about Christ' assumes the Isaianic theology of the word as the living, active agent of divine purpose; it is not the hearer's response that generates faith but the word itself, which accomplishes God's redemptive intention.

18.note: 'Their voice has gone out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world' — David's hymn to creation's witness: the heavens declare God's glory wordlessly yet universally. Paul cites this as evidence that Israel 'has heard' — the cosmic reach of the word makes the claim of ignorance implausible; what was true of creation's witness is now true of the gospel's proclamation.
Their voice has gone out into all the earth

19.note: 'I will make you envious by those who are not a nation; I will make you angry by a nation that has no understanding' — the Song of Moses, in which God declares he will provoke Israel to jealousy through a 'not-nation' (lo-am), is Paul's proof that Gentile inclusion was the means by which God would deal with Israel's unbelief. Moses named the pattern; Paul sees it unfolding.
I will make you envious by a nation not a nation

20.note: 'I was found by those who did not seek me; I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me' — Isaiah's oracle in which God announces his accessibility to those who had no prior covenant claim. Paul reads this as describing the Gentiles: they were not pursuing God, yet found him; the reversal of expected advantage is the Isaianic pattern for what is now happening in the mission to the nations.
Found by those who did not seek me
Found by those who did not seek me

21.note: 'All day long I have held out my hands to a disobedient and obstinate people' — the counterpoint to Isaiah 65:1: while Gentiles find God without seeking, God extends his hands all day to Israel who refuses. Paul places this at the chapter's close as the poignant Isaianic summary of Israel's condition: the initiative is divine, the resistance persistent, and yet the hands remain extended.
All day I held out my hands to a disobedient people
All day long I held out my hands to a disobedient people

1.note: The Psalm's assurance that 'the LORD will not reject his people; he will never forsake his inheritance' supplies the theological warrant for Paul's emphatic 'By no means!' — God's covenant with Israel is not merely a human claim but a divine pledge grounded in the nature of covenant loyalty.

Samuel's assurance after Israel's sin in demanding a king — 'For the sake of his great name the LORD will not reject his people, because the LORD was pleased to make you his own' — establishes the same logic Paul invokes: Israel's continued standing rests on God's name and purpose, not Israel's performance.

Samuel declares that the LORD will not reject his people for the sake of his great name — Paul's 'Did God reject his people? By no means!' stands in the tradition of the OT assurance that divine rejection of the covenant people is incompatible with God's own faithfulness to his name; Paul's own Israelite identity is an argument from lived experience.

2.note: The language of God 'foreknowing' (prognōsis) carries the weight of the prophetic commissioning vocabulary — to foreknow is to elect and form for a purpose before historical action. Jeremiah's 'before I formed you in the womb I knew you' establishes this pattern: divine foreknowledge entails covenantal commitment, not mere omniscience.

Paul directs readers to 'the passage about Elijah' — a Jewish way of citing a scriptural section by its protagonist — so the entire Elijah narrative (1 Kgs 17–19) provides the frame: apostasy, apparent extinction of the faithful remnant, and God's hidden preservation become the template for understanding Israel's present situation.

Elijah's complaint begins the Elijah-remnant section Paul introduces in v3 — the verse anticipates the Elijah passage Paul will cite; God did not reject his people is the answer to Elijah's despair, confirmed by the 7,000 reserved of v4.

3.note: Paul quotes Elijah's complaint to God verbatim: 'Lord, they have killed your prophets and torn down your altars; I am the only one left, and they are trying to kill me.' The complaint functions as the nadir of prophetic discouragement — the point at which the prophet believes covenant Israel has effectively ceased to exist.
Elijah's complaint — I am the only one left

4.note: God's reply to Elijah — 'I have reserved for myself seven thousand who have not bowed the knee to Baal' — is the scriptural paradigm for remnant preservation: the faithful are not visible to human inventory but are known and held by God. The 'for myself' (emautō) is emphatic in both the Hebrew and Paul's Greek, stressing divine initiative in preservation.
Seven thousand reserved who have not bowed to Baal

5.note: Isaiah's prophecy that 'a remnant of Israel, the survivors of the house of Jacob, will no longer rely on him who struck them... a remnant will return, a remnant of Jacob, to the Mighty God' — the 'remnant chosen by grace' Paul identifies in the present church echoes this prophetic remnant structure: small, grace-preserved, but real.

Isaiah's aside that 'unless the LORD Almighty had left us some survivors, we would have become like Sodom' frames the remnant not as Israel's achievement but as God's merciful restraint from total judgment — the category Paul invokes when he locates a remnant 'at the present time.'

6.note: God's promise to act 'not for your sake, O house of Israel, but for the sake of my holy name' — the grace/works antithesis Paul articulates here has its OT precedent in Ezekiel's repeated insistence that Israel's restoration is grounded in God's covenantal name-honor, not in any merit Israel can contribute.

Moses explicitly warns that Israel is entering the land 'not because of your righteousness or your integrity' but because of the nations' wickedness and God's oath to the patriarchs — the foundational OT demolition of works-based election that Paul's grace/works antithesis here presupposes.

God chose Israel not because of their numbers or merit but because he loved them and kept his oath — the Deuteronomic grounding of election in grace rather than merit is the OT basis for Paul's insistence that if the remnant is by grace, works cannot be the criterion; the Deuteronomic love-choice already excluded merit as the basis of divine selection.

7.note: Moses tells Israel 'to this day the LORD has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear' — Paul's 'the others were hardened' (epōrōthēsan) draws on this Deuteronomic pattern: judicial blindness as God's response to persistent covenant failure, not an arbitrary imposition.

Isaiah's oracle that God poured out on Israel 'a spirit of deep sleep, and has closed your eyes (the prophets), and your heads (the seers) he has covered' — the hardening Paul identifies as the present condition of non-believing Israel has prophetic precedent in Isaiah's own generation.

God telling Isaiah to make the people's heart calloused, their ears dull, their eyes closed — the hardening of the majority of Israel that Paul describes in the elect versus the rest is anticipated in Isaiah's own commission, where he was told the effect of his preaching would be hardening rather than repentance.

8.note: Paul quotes the LXX of Isaiah 29:10: 'God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear.' The Isaianic context is Judah's reliance on Egypt and failure to consult God — a pattern of covenant unfaithfulness that results in a judicial incapacity to perceive God's working.

Paul's composite citation blends Isaiah 29:10 with Deuteronomy 29:4 ('the LORD has not given you a mind that understands, or eyes that see, or ears that hear'). Moses speaks this at the end of the wilderness period, identifying a hardening that persisted through forty years of miracle — Paul applies both texts together to characterize Israel's current condition.
God gave them a spirit of stupor — eyes that do not see

9.note: Paul quotes David's imprecatory psalm: 'May their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution for them; may their eyes be darkened so they cannot see, and their backs be bent forever.' That David wrote this psalm in his own distress and it is now applied to Israel's hardening signals that the pattern of rejection-and-stumbling appears within covenant history itself.
May their table become a snare and a trap

10.note: The second half of the Davidic citation (vv.9-10 span a single quote from Psalms 69:22-23): 'May their eyes be darkened so they cannot see, and their backs be bent forever.' The bent-back image connotes permanent servile burden — Paul frames Israel's judicial hardening in the language of the rejected psalmist's own cry for divine action against his persecutors.

The continuation of the same psalm — may their backs be bent forever — concludes Paul's citation; the bowed-back image of perpetual servitude is applied to Israel's present condition of spiritual submission to the works-righteousness system that cannot justify.

11.note: The Song of Moses predicts that God will make Israel jealous 'by those who are not a people' and provoke them to anger 'by a foolish nation.' Paul's statement that salvation came to the Gentiles 'to make Israel envious' is a direct application of this Mosaic prophetic pattern, presented as God's intention embedded in Torah itself.

12.note: Isaiah envisions a future when 'Jacob will take root, Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit' — Paul's logic (if their transgression means riches for the world, how much more their fullness?) mirrors this trajectory: Israel's eschatological restoration will be a blessing that overflows to all nations, not only to Israel itself.

Jacob will take root and Israel will bud and blossom and fill the world with fruit — Paul's 'if their transgression means riches for the world, and their loss means riches for the Gentiles, how much greater riches will their fullness bring' anticipates the a-fortiori logic using the Isaianic vine-image of Israel filling the world with abundance when restored.

13.note: God addresses the Servant: 'I will also make you a light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth.' Paul's identification of himself as 'the apostle to the Gentiles' locates his ministry within the Isaianic Servant commission — his boasting in his ministry is not pride but recognition that the Servant's task is being carried out through him.

14.note: Paul's stated goal of arousing his own people to envy is the deliberate fulfillment of Moses's prediction in the Song of Moses that God would use a 'not-a-people' to make Israel jealous. What Moses presented as God's judicial strategy in Deut 32 Paul now consciously enacts as his apostolic mission.

15.note: Isaiah depicts the nations bringing Israel's brothers as a 'grain offering to the LORD' from all nations, suggesting the Gentile mission itself serves the purpose of Israel's eschatological gathering. Paul's 'what will their acceptance be but life from the dead?' fits this trajectory: Israel's full reception is the consummating event of redemptive history.

The valley of dry bones — Israel as a 'whole house' brought back from a death-like exile — provides the nearest OT analogy for 'life from the dead': restoration from a state of judicial non-existence to renewed covenant life. Paul's phrase reaches for this eschatological register without direct quotation.

16.note: The law required offering the first portion of ground grain (challah) to the LORD, with the holiness of that firstfruits portion consecrating the whole batch. Paul uses this priestly logic to argue that if the first Jewish believers (the remnant) are holy, the whole of Israel shares the consecrated status — holiness flows from root to branches within the same covenantal lump.

God named Israel 'a thriving olive tree with fruit beautiful in form' — but then declared its branches burned because of the evil it had done. Paul's olive-tree image activates this Jeremianic picture; the natural branches that are broken off are those Jeremiah foresaw being judged, and the tree itself (the patriarchal covenant) remains intact.

The firstfruits dough offering that consecrates the whole batch — Paul's 'if the part of the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, then the whole batch is holy' applies the Levitical principle of firstfruits-consecration to argue that the patriarchs (and the believing remnant) as firstfruits sanctify and anticipate the consecration of all Israel.

17.note: Jeremiah's image of Israel as an olive tree whose branches are broken — 'with the roar of a mighty storm he will set it on fire, and its branches will be broken' — supplies the governing metaphor Paul develops through vv.17-24. The breaking is judicial, the tree covenantal, and the Gentile graft an unexpected reversal of the same divine agency that broke the natural branches.

God's promise that restored Israel 'will blossom like a lily' and 'his shoots will spread' with the image of an olive tree — 'his splendor will be like an olive tree' — provides a positive counterpart to Jeremiah's broken tree, suggesting that the olive-tree metaphor carries both judgment and restoration potential in the prophetic tradition.

The psalmist as a green olive tree in the house of God — Paul's olive-tree image for Israel and the Gentile wild-olive shoot inhabits the OT's consistent use of the olive tree as a symbol of covenant blessing and divine presence; the psalmist's identity with a flourishing olive tree is the positive image whose disruption Paul describes in the broken-off branches.

18.note: God addresses Israel as 'you whom I took from the ends of the earth... the offspring of Abraham, my friend... I have chosen you and have not rejected you.' Paul warns Gentile believers not to be arrogant toward the natural branches precisely because the root — Abraham's election and the patriarchal promise — precedes and sustains the Gentile graft; the Gentile does not support the root.

The LORD called Israel a thriving olive tree, but announced that its branches would be burned — Jeremiah's prior use of the olive tree for Israel as both flourishing and subject to covenant judgment is the background for Paul's grafting-in language; the same tree can shed branches through covenant faithlessness.

19.note: God announces in Zion a 'tested stone, a precious cornerstone for a sure foundation' — and adds 'the one who relies on it will never be stricken with panic.' Paul ventriloquizes a Gentile Christian objection ('branches were broken off so that I could be grafted in') that shows a misreading of election as competitive exclusion rather than sequential inclusion.

Israel chosen from all peoples to be his treasured possession — the Gentile believer who reasons that branches were broken off so they could be grafted in is implicitly claiming Israel's election-status for themselves; Paul's warning in v20 will correct the arrogance that assumes Gentile inclusion displaces Jewish election.

20.note: The warning 'do not be arrogant, but tremble' continues the Deuteronomy 32 frame: the same Song of Moses that predicted Israel's jealousy of the Gentiles also predicts God's own jealousy over Israel — the pattern of judgment and mercy is reversible. Gentile arrogance would replicate the very failure that led to the natural branches' breaking.

God declares 'these are the ones I look on with favor: those who are humble and contrite in spirit, and who tremble at my word' — Paul's call for trembling rather than arrogance has an Isaianic resonance, where the right posture before God's sovereign action is reverence, not presumption on one's chosen status.

Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall — Paul's 'do not be arrogant but be afraid' draws on the wisdom tradition's consistent warning that the pride of the secure is the precondition for their fall; the Gentile believer who boasts over broken-off branches stands in the position Proverbs repeatedly warns against.

21.note: Jeremiah's olive-tree passage makes the breaking of Israel's branches an act of divine judgment for covenant unfaithfulness — Paul's warning 'if God did not spare the natural branches, he will not spare you either' applies the same judicial logic to Gentile believers who abandon faith. The olive tree is God's covenant tree; branches stand only by the terms the tree-owner sets.

The parable of the vine transplanted — Ezekiel's allegory of a vine torn up when it forsakes its planting ground is the OT background for Paul's warning that God did not spare the natural branches; the same God who cut the vine in Ezekiel's parable will not spare the wild-olive Gentile who becomes arrogant.

22.note: God's anguished declaration 'How can I give you up, Ephraim? How can I hand you over, Israel?... My heart is changed within me; all my compassion is aroused' captures the same divine ambivalence Paul articulates as 'kindness and sternness.' The sternness executes judgment (Hosea 11:5-7); the kindness overrides destruction — God's character holds both simultaneously.

The Lamentations poet's insight that 'the Lord does not reject forever; though he brings grief, he will show compassion, so great is his unfailing love. For he does not willingly bring affliction or grief to anyone' — the sternness Paul names is real but not God's final or preferred word; it serves the larger purpose of mercy.

The gentleness of the divine shepherd — Paul's 'consider the kindness and sternness of God' names both qualities that Isaiah's portraits of God combine; kindness (chrestotes) toward those who receive him and severity (apotomia) toward those who fall away are both true of the same God.

23.note: God's promise to Israel — 'I will heal their waywardness and love them freely, for my anger has turned away from them... they will flourish like grain... and blossom like the vine' — is the prophetic warrant for Paul's claim that God 'is able to graft them in again.' The restoration of unbelieving Israel is not hypothetical but grounded in prophetic promise already on the table.

God's promise to pour out 'a spirit of grace and supplication' on the house of David, after which they will 'look on me, the one they have pierced, and they will mourn' — Paul's confidence in Israel's future grafting-in is underpinned by prophetic passages like this that anticipate a national turning to God prompted by divine initiative, not human decision.

God able to make the dry bones live again — Paul's 'God is able to graft them in again' appeals to the divine omnipotence that makes Israel's future restoration not merely wishful but guaranteed by the power of the God who raises the dead; Ezekiel's resurrection-of-Israel vision is the OT ground for this claim.

24.note: The olive tree Paul has been developing since v.17 is Jeremiah's image of Israel — and Jeremiah's own tree is a cultivated olive tree that Israel once was (thriving) and whose branches were broken in judgment. Paul's argument that wild shoots grafted into a cultivated tree is an unnatural reversal concedes the anomaly: the Gentile mission is a greater miracle than Israel's own restoration would be.

God gathering Israel a second time from the four quarters of the earth — the re-grafting of the natural branches is the Pauline metaphor for the Isaianic second gathering; what is contrary to nature (v24) from a horticultural standpoint is entirely consistent with God's covenant purposes announced in the prophets.

25.note: Isaiah's commission to 'make the heart of this people calloused; make their ears dull and close their eyes' — the judicial hardening God imposed on Isaiah's generation — is the prophetic prototype for the 'partial hardening' Paul identifies as a present-tense mystery. The hardening is temporary ('until the full number of Gentiles has come in') and purposive, as in Isaiah's own context.

The Elijah narrative Paul cited at the chapter's opening (vv.2-4) forms the bookend: then as now, Israel appears to have failed entirely, but God preserves a remnant. The partial hardening is the same kind of divine mystery Elijah could not see from his vantage point — 'I am the only one left' — but God holds.

The hardening of Israel until the cities lie ruined — Paul's mystery that hardening has come to part of Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in echoes Isaiah's own commission to preach until the land is utterly desolate (until the stump remains, Isaiah 6:13); the hardening has a temporal limit in Isaiah just as in Paul.

26.note: Paul cites the LXX of Isaiah 59:20-21 ('The deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob; and this is my covenant with them') as the scriptural warrant for 'all Israel will be saved.' He reads the Isaianic Redeemer-from-Zion as the Christ event, with the covenant-of-forgiveness as the eschatological completion of Israel's salvation history. Note: Paul's 'from Zion' (ek Siōn) differs from MT 'to Zion' (lĕṣîyôn), likely harmonized with Psalms 14:7 / Romans 53:6.

Isaiah's prophecy that 'Israel will be saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation; you will never be put to shame or disgraced, to ages everlasting' — Paul's 'all Israel will be saved' reaches for this unconditional register of final, permanent restoration that goes beyond any partial restoration Israel experienced before the Messiah.

The deliverer will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who repent of their sins — Paul's 'the deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob' (adapting the LXX) reads the Isaianic Zion-deliverer as the returning Christ whose parousia will precipitate Israel's final salvation; the modification from 'to Zion' to 'from Zion' reflects the post-resurrection, post-ascension location of the deliverer.

Jacob's guilt will be atoned for when all the altar stones are to be like chalk — Paul's 'and this is my covenant with them when I take away their sins' (v27) draws on Isaiah 27:9's promise of Israel's final atonement; the eschatological removal of sin is the content of the new covenant applied to Israel's ultimate salvation.
The deliverer will come from Zion
The deliverer will come from Zion and remove ungodliness

27.note: Isaiah states 'by this, then, will Jacob's guilt be atoned for, and this will be the full fruit of the removal of his sin' — the covenant for the removal of sins that Paul quotes belongs to this stream of Isaianic expectation that Israel's final atonement is God's own act, anticipated but not yet fully accomplished in the old covenant sacrificial system.

The new covenant promise — God writing his law on hearts and forgetting Israel's sins ('I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more') — is the same covenant Paul identifies in the Isa 59 citation as coming with the Redeemer. Paul reads these Isaianic and Jeremianic covenant texts as the same eschatological moment.

28.note: Moses grounds Israel's election not in their numbers or greatness but in the LORD's love and his oath to the patriarchs — 'because the LORD loved you and kept the oath he swore to your ancestors.' Paul's 'as far as election is concerned, they are loved on account of the patriarchs' reproduces this exact logic: Israel's present beloved status derives from God's patriarchal commitments, not current faithfulness.

Moses's intercession appeals to God to 'remember your servants Abraham, Isaac and Israel, to whom you swore by your own self' — the patriarchal oath as an irrevocable constraint on divine action with Israel. Paul's argument that Israel remains 'beloved for the sake of the patriarchs' stands in the same intercessory tradition.

The faithful God keeping his covenant love to a thousand generations of those who love him — Paul's 'as far as election is concerned, they are loved on account of the patriarchs' grounds the irrevocability of Israel's election in the Deuteronomic divine faithfulness to the covenant made with the patriarchs; the thousands-of-generations promise cannot be annulled by Israel's current unbelief.

29.note: Balaam's oracle: 'God is not human, that he should lie, not a human being, that he should change his mind. Does he speak and then not act? Does he promise and not fulfill?' — Paul's declaration that 'God's gifts and his call are irrevocable' (ametamelēta) is the logical extension of this divine immutability: once given, the covenant cannot be recalled.

God's oath about the Davidic covenant — 'I will not violate my covenant or alter what my lips have uttered. Once for all, I have sworn by my holiness' — establishes that divine covenant commitments function as self-binding oaths. Paul applies this immutability principle to the wider Abrahamic election.

30.note: Hosea's prophecy that where Israel was told 'you are not my people,' they will be called 'children of the living God' — Paul's description of Gentiles as those who 'at one time disobedient to God have now received mercy' is the enacted fulfillment of Hosea's vision of those-not-a-people being incorporated as God's people.

God's promise 'I will show my love to the one I called Not my loved one. I will say to those called Not my people, You are my people' — the disobedience-to-mercy movement Paul describes in vv.30-31 echoes this Hoseanic oscillation where rejection becomes the occasion for a more surprising mercy.

Lo-Ruhamah (not loved) and Lo-Ammi (not my people) reversed in Hosea to Ruhamah (loved) and Ammi (my people) — Paul's chiastic structure (formerly disobedient → now received mercy; now disobedient → will receive mercy) mirrors Hosea's reversal pattern; mercy following disobedience is the structural principle of both Hosea's covenant theology and Paul's argument.

31.note: The symmetrical mercy-structure Paul constructs (Gentiles receive mercy through Israel's disobedience; Israel will receive mercy through Gentile mercy) echoes Hosea's reversal of 'Not my people' into 'my people' — not merely for one party but as a pattern of divine mercy that operates through apparent rejection toward deeper inclusion.

God pouring out a spirit of grace and supplication on the house of David so they will look on the one they have pierced — the future receptivity of Israel that Paul anticipates ('so they too may now receive mercy') connects to Zechariah's vision of Israel's grief-unto-repentance when they recognize the pierced one; Zechariah's prophecy is one OT anchor for Paul's expectation.

32.note: Isaiah's diagnosis that 'we all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way' — Paul's 'God has bound everyone over to disobedience so that he may have mercy on them all' generalizes this universal straying into a divine strategy: the universal fall is the precondition for the universal scope of mercy.

David's plea 'do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one living is righteous before you' — Paul's binding of all to disobedience is the judicial statement that no one has a claim on God's favor; what follows (mercy on all) is therefore entirely grace, as David already understood in his penitential register.

God declaring that humanity had become like one of us knowing good and evil — the Adamic condition (bound over to disobedience, knowing good but choosing evil) is the universal situation Paul summarizes; God's binding all in disobedience so that he may have mercy on all is the reversal of the Adamic fall through the mercy that operates at the same universal scope as the fall.

33.note: Zophar's question 'Can you fathom the mysteries of God? Can you probe the limits of the Almighty? They are higher than the heavens — what can you do? They are deeper than the depths of the realm of the dead — what can you know?' — Paul's doxology reaches for the same unsearchable-depth register, transposing it into praise rather than rebuke.

God's declaration that 'my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways... as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways' — Paul's 'his paths beyond tracing out' (anexichniastoi) is a compressed allusion to this Isaianic divine transcendence that stands behind the entire argument of chapters 9-11.

Can you fathom the mysteries of God? They are higher than the heavens and deeper than Sheol — the doxological wonder at divine inscrutableness with which Paul closes the argument of Romans 9-11 inhabits the wisdom tradition of Job; the depths and heights of God's wisdom that Job's friends gesticulate toward are now displayed in the mystery of election, hardening, and universal mercy.

34.note: Paul quotes the LXX of Isaiah 40:13: 'Who has known the mind of the LORD? Or who has been his counselor?' In Isaiah, the question introduces the incomparable Sovereign who needs no advisor and whose creation-governance no human can superintend. Paul deploys it to close the argument of chapters 9-11: the mystery of Israel's partial hardening and future salvation is not accessible to human advisory oversight — only to worship.

Eliphaz asks Job: do you limit wisdom to yourself? Have you listened in on God's deliberations? — Paul's 'who has been his counselor' echoes the wisdom-tradition's consistent warning against presuming to have access to the divine council; the rhetorical questions in vv34-35 draw on this theme.
Who has known the mind of the Lord?
Who has known the mind of the Lord?

35.note: Paul quotes Job 41:11 LXX: 'Who has given to God first, that God should repay them?' In the Job context, God challenges Job from the whirlwind: 'Everything under heaven belongs to me.' The question demolishes the notion that God is indebted to any creature — which, applied to chapters 9-11, means that God owes Israel nothing beyond what covenant grace freely provides.

God declaring: who has given to me that I must repay? Everything under heaven belongs to me — Paul's 'who has ever given to God, that God should repay them?' is a near-quotation of Job 41:11; the divine self-sufficiency that God asserts from the whirlwind is the theological ground for understanding mercy as pure grace, not compensation.

36.note: God declares 'I am the first and I am the last; apart from me there is no God' — Paul's triadic formula 'from him and through him and for him are all things' is a compressed monotheistic doxology in the same tradition: all of reality's origin, sustaining, and goal are in God alone, which is why the unsearchable ways of chapters 9-11 terminate in worship rather than complaint.

The Psalm's declaration 'the earth is the LORD's, and everything in it' provides the OT grounding for Paul's doxological claim that all things are 'from him and through him and for him.' If all things belong to God, then his sovereign management of Jewish and Gentile history in Romans 9-11 is simply the Creator exercising his proprietary prerogative.

David's prayer — 'Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hand' — Paul's 'for from him and through him and for him are all things' is the doxological formulation of the same Davidic insight: creation and creature exist within the divine self-giving, not beside or independent of it.

1.note: David's insight that 'the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart you, God, will not despise' — Paul's 'living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God' extends the Psalmist's logic: the whole bodily self offered in ongoing commitment is the sacrifice God values beyond burnt offerings. The 'living' replaces the animal's death as the mode of presentation.

Isaiah envisions the nations bringing Israel's brothers as 'an offering to the LORD' from all the ends of the earth — Paul's summons for believers to offer their bodies 'as a living sacrifice' draws on this Isaianic language of persons as the true offering, transposing it from a future Gentile mission to a present ethical imperative.

The whole burnt offering presented before the LORD — Paul's 'living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God' uses Levitical sacrificial categories (holy, pleasing, offered as worship) but transforms them: the sacrifice is living, continuous, and constituted by the self-presentation of the entire person rather than the death of an animal.

2.note: The blessed person who 'does not walk in step with the wicked' and whose 'delight is in the law of the LORD' and who 'meditates on his law day and night' — Paul's 'do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind' is the equivalent positive command, placing Torah-meditation's transforming function now within the Christ-shaped renewal of the mind.

God's call to 'seek the LORD while he may be found' paired with the revelation that his thoughts and ways are not human thoughts and ways — Paul's 'then you will be able to test and approve what God's will is' presents transformed discernment as the outcome of mind-renewal, the human capacity to perceive divine good will that Isa 55 presents as inaccessible to unreformed human reasoning.

God's promise to give his people a new heart and a new spirit, removing the heart of stone — Paul's 'be transformed by the renewing of your mind' is the apostolic exhortation corresponding to the Ezekielian promise; the new-covenant transformation of the inner person is both divine gift (Ezekiel) and ethical summons (Paul), inseparable poles of the same reality.

3.note: The prophet's summary of covenant obligation — 'to act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God' — undergirds Paul's call to sober self-assessment. The humble walk Micah requires is the existential posture Paul translates into not thinking of oneself more highly than one ought: humility before God and neighbor is the covenant's basic anthropological requirement.

Proverbs' antithesis 'when pride comes, then comes disgrace, but with humility comes wisdom' — Paul's call to 'sober judgment' (sōphrosynē) parallels the Wisdom tradition's linkage of accurate self-knowledge to humility: only the person not deceived about their own greatness can exercise the wise discernment the body of Christ requires.

Do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD and shun evil — Paul's 'do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but think with sober judgment' is the Solomonic wisdom applied to the new covenant community; self-knowledge that includes awareness of divine grace-measure echoes the wisdom tradition's consistent critique of self-inflation.

4.note: God instructs Moses to gather seventy elders and promises to 'take some of the power of the Spirit that is on you and put it on them' so the burden is distributed — the many-members-one-body structure Paul articulates has its antecedent in this Mosaic distribution of Spirit-gifted leadership, where diversity of function serves the single community.

The body-as-community image Paul develops here draws on OT corporate anthropology — Israel as a single body, members with distinct functions — but Paul's innovation is the 'in Christ' qualifier that makes the body the body of the Messiah, the new-covenant assembly that replaces the tribal assembly of the Sinai covenant.

5.note: God's command to join two sticks into one in Ezekiel's sign-act — 'they will become one in your hand' as a sign of reunited Israel — prefigures Paul's 'though many, form one body in Christ, and each member belongs to all the others.' The one-body-from-many is a covenantal category that Ezekiel dramatized as the eschatological restoration of divided Israel into unified covenant people.

Israel as a kingdom of priests — Paul's 'in Christ we, though many, form one body' applies the Sinai corporate identity to the new covenant community, which inherits the priestly-national identity of Israel now constituted by union with Christ rather than by Mosaic covenant.

6.note: Isaiah's vision of the Spirit of the LORD resting on the Messianic branch — 'the Spirit of wisdom and of understanding, the Spirit of counsel and of might, the Spirit of the knowledge and fear of the LORD' — presents distributed spiritual endowments as the characteristic gift of the messianic age. Paul's 'gifts, according to the grace given to each of us' places the community of Christ within this Spirit-distributed-gifts framework.

The Spirit distributed among the seventy elders, each prophesying — Paul's 'we have different gifts, according to the grace given to each of us' stands in the tradition of the distributed Spirit in Numbers 11, where the divine Spirit-endowment for various functions was understood as a single Spirit distributing different capacities to different members of the community.

7.note: The Levitical service (ʿavōdāh) — structured, assigned, and given as calling from God — provides the background for Paul's 'if it is serving, then serve.' Paul's word for serving (diakonia) echoes the Levitical conception of ministry as a specific calling to be carried out faithfully within the community of God's people, not as a hierarchical status but as God-assigned function.

Ezra devoted to studying, doing, and teaching the law — the teaching office Paul mentions (if it is teaching, then teach) stands in the OT tradition of the teacher-scribe whose function was to transmit and apply the covenant word; in the new covenant the teacher transmits the apostolic gospel.

8.note: Proverbs' paradox 'one person gives freely, yet gains even more; another withholds unduly, but comes to poverty. A generous person will prosper; whoever refreshes others will be refreshed' — Paul's call to give 'with generosity' (haplotēs) is shaped by the Wisdom tradition's conviction that extravagant giving, not cautious calculation, is both the commanded and the fruitful pattern.

9.note: Amos's command 'hate evil, love good; maintain justice in the courts' — Paul's 'hate what is evil; cling to what is good' is a compressed echo of this prophetic imperative. Amos addressed a community that had separated covenant ritual from covenant ethics; Paul reasserts the same requirement for the Roman community: affective commitment to good must be unsimulated (anypokritos).

The Psalm addresses those who 'love the LORD' and commands them to 'hate evil' — Paul's pairing of sincere love with hatred of evil replicates this Psalmic linkage where covenant loyalty to God and moral abhorrence of evil are not separate obligations but a single integrated response.

10.note: The command 'love your neighbor as yourself' (which Paul will quote explicitly in Romans 13:9) provides the ground for 'be devoted to one another in brotherly love' (philadelphia). The covenant love-command is the source code Paul unpacks throughout Romans 12-13 as specific communal practices.

The person who dwells on God's holy mountain 'honors those who fear the LORD' — Paul's 'honor one another above yourselves' picks up the Psalmic category of honor-within-the-covenant-community as a mark of those fit to approach God.

11.note: David's declaration 'zeal for your house consumes me' — the kind of consuming devotion to God's purposes that exhausts itself in service — provides the register for Paul's 'never be lacking in zeal, but keep your spiritual fervor, serving the Lord.' Zeal is not an optional intensity add-on but the covenant posture appropriate to those serving the God of Israel.

The ant who works without being commanded, never lacking because of its diligence — Paul's 'never be lacking in zeal; keep your spiritual fervor' draws on the wisdom tradition's commendation of diligent labor applied here to the spiritual life; sloth in serving the Lord is the new covenant form of the sluggard's failure.

12.note: Jeremiah's Lamentations — 'The LORD is good to those whose hope is in him, to the one who seeks him; it is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD' — provides the template for 'joyful in hope, patient in affliction.' The patience Paul commands is specifically covenant patience: not stoic endurance but hope in the God who saves within history.

The Psalmist's urgent prayer 'when I call, answer me quickly; do not hide your face from me' — the faithfulness in prayer Paul commands is not bare persistence but the trusting address to a God who has historically answered his people. Prayer in affliction is the covenantal response, not the alternative to hope.

I wait for the LORD more than watchmen wait for the morning — Paul's 'joyful in hope, patient in affliction, faithful in prayer' is the dispositional triad that the Psalter's lament-and-hope tradition produces: the hope that waits, the patience that endures affliction, and the prayer that maintains the relationship during trial.

13.note: Moses commands 'if anyone is poor among your fellow Israelites... do not be hardhearted or tightfisted toward them. Rather, be openhanded and freely lend them whatever they need.' Paul's 'share with the Lord's people who are in need' extends this covenant obligation of economic solidarity from ethnic Israel to the wider body of Christ.

Abraham's immediate and extravagant hospitality to three strangers — running to meet them, preparing a feast — becomes the paradigm for 'practice hospitality' (philoxenian diōkein, lit. 'pursue love-of-strangers'). The Abraham narrative establishes that the stranger-welcome is a covenantal act, not a social nicety.

Do not be hardhearted toward your poor brother; open your hand freely — Paul's 'share with the Lord's people who are in need' continues the Deuteronomic open-handedness command, now applied to the new covenant community of the poor.

14.note: The Proverbs principle — give your enemy food and water — provides the background ethic Paul both alludes to here and quotes directly in v.20. The blessing of persecutors extends this Wisdom-tradition non-retaliation into active benevolence: not merely withholding harm but actively invoking God's good on those who harm.

God's reversal of Balaam's curse into blessing ('the LORD your God turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the LORD your God loves you') — the underlying principle that divine blessing can override human cursing provides the theological ground for the command to bless and not curse; the believer aligns with God's own blessing-posture toward the enemy.

If your enemy is hungry, give him food; heap burning coals on his head — Paul's 'bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse' is the NT intensification of the Proverbs principle of active good toward enemies; the curse-tradition of the OT (as in Ps 109) is here replaced by the blessing-posture that Proverbs already points toward.

15.note: Job's self-defense includes 'have I not wept for those in trouble? Has not my soul grieved for the poor?' — mourning with those who mourn is presented not as emotional solidarity alone but as covenantal compassion, the human reflection of God's own mourning over the afflicted (Isaiah 63:9 'in all their distress he too was distressed').

David describes how he 'went about mourning as though for my friend or brother. I bowed my head in grief as though weeping for my mother' — empathic solidarity with the suffering of others is the Psalmic mark of covenant faithfulness, not emotional performance. Paul's command to mourn with mourners inherits this covenantal register.

Job weeping for those in trouble — the empathetic co-suffering Paul commends in 'rejoice with those who rejoice; mourn with those who mourn' is the covenant-community practice that the wisdom tradition values; Job's weeping for others' suffering is the OT form of the communal empathy Paul commands.

16.note: Proverbs' warning 'do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD and shun evil' — Paul's 'do not be conceited' (mē ginesthe phronimoi par' heautois) is a near-quotation of the LXX, grounding the call to communal harmony in the Wisdom tradition's fundamental anthropological principle: self-regarded wisdom is the root of social fracture.

Isaiah's woe oracle against 'those who are wise in their own eyes and clever in their own sight' — Paul's prohibition of self-wise conceit echoes this prophetic indictment, placing the community's peaceable life in direct contrast to the self-regarding posture that Isaiah named as a marker of covenant failure.

17.note: The Wisdom command 'do not say I'll pay you back for this wrong. Wait for the LORD, and he will avenge you' — Paul's 'do not repay anyone evil for evil' operates within this Proverbial framework: restraint from retaliation is not passivity but the deliberate transfer of the revenge mandate to God, who alone can execute it justly.

The instruction to 'win favor and a good name in the sight of God and man' — Paul's 'be careful to do what is right in the eyes of everyone' participates in the Wisdom tradition's concern that covenant behavior be publicly legible, not merely internally motivated.

18.note: The Psalm commands 'seek peace and pursue it' (diōxon eirēnēn in the LXX) — Paul's 'if it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone' inherits both the imperative and the realism of the Psalmist's formulation: peace is to be actively sought (pursued, not merely not-broken), even if not always achievable.

19.note: Paul quotes the Song of Moses — 'It is mine to avenge; I will repay, says the Lord' — to ground the prohibition on private revenge. In the Deuteronomy context, God's exclusive claim to vengeance is part of his jealous superintendence of Israel's history; Paul extends it to the Christian community: precisely because God owns the vengeance mandate, believers must vacate it entirely.
It is mine to avenge; I will repay

20.note: Paul quotes the LXX of Proverbs 25:21-22 — 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink. In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head.' The 'burning coals' image in its ancient Near Eastern context may signify a gesture of remorse-inducing generosity (carrying coals to light a fire as an act of conciliation) rather than further shaming; Paul frames the whole as 'overcoming evil with good' in v.21.
If your enemy is hungry, feed him — heap burning coals

21.note: The Psalmist's dual command to 'turn from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it' anticipates Paul's closing summary of Romans 12's ethics — 'do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.' The active pursuit of good is not merely defense against evil but the covenant community's positive posture toward the world around it.

Sin crouching at Cain's door, desiring to have him but he must rule over it — Paul's 'do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good' echoes the divine summons to Cain (who failed); the community can succeed where Cain failed by meeting evil with good rather than escalating to greater evil.

1.note: Daniel's declaration that God 'changes times and seasons; he deposes kings and raises up others' — Paul's assertion that 'there is no authority except that which God has established' rests on the same biblical-theological premise: political authority is not self-grounding but is installed and removed within God's providential governance of history.

Wisdom declares 'by me kings reign and rulers issue decrees that are just; by me princes govern, and nobles — all who rule on earth.' The governing authorities Paul addresses exist not as autonomous human achievements but as participants in Wisdom's ordering of political life — a created function within God's governance, not a rival to it.

2.note: Samuel's condemnation of Saul — 'rebellion is like the sin of divination, and arrogance like the evil of idolatry' — establishes the gravity of resisting divinely-ordered authority. Paul's 'those who do so will bring judgment on themselves' has roots in this Samueline judgment: resistance to God's ordering of authority is not merely political crime but a spiritual category.

Korah's rebellion against Moses's divinely appointed authority — those who rebel against God's appointed leaders rebel against God himself; Paul's 'whoever rebels against the authority is rebelling against what God has instituted' applies the Mosaic-Korah principle to civil governance.

3.note: The royal Psalm envisions the ideal king who 'will defend the afflicted among the people and save the children of the needy; he will crush the oppressor.' Paul's description of rulers as 'holding no terror for those who do right' reflects this Psalmic theology of delegated governance: the magistrate's terror falls on wrongdoers, not the righteous — and where it does not, the ruler has failed his divine mandate.

A king's favor is toward a wise servant, but his wrath falls on a shameful one — the OT principle that legitimate authority rewards good conduct and punishes bad is the wisdom background for Paul's 'rulers hold no terror for those who do right, but for those who do wrong'; civil governance functions by the same principle of rewarding good and punishing evil that wisdom literature consistently teaches.

4.note: God addresses Cyrus as his anointed instrument: 'I will go before you and will level the mountains... I have called you by name... though you do not acknowledge me.' The pagan ruler as God's 'servant' (diakonos theou) in Paul's language echoes this Isaianic pattern of God using non-believing rulers to accomplish redemptive-historical purposes.

God declares 'I will give all your countries into the hands of my servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon' — the most uncomfortable OT case of a violent pagan ruler designated God's 'servant' for a specific political purpose. Paul's use of diakonos theou for the governing authority carries this same Jeremianic weight: even the sword of an unjust state operates within God's framework.

Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed — the Noahic grounding of capital punishment in the image of God establishes that the sword-bearing authority Paul mentions ('for God's servant is an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer') has OT precedent; civil authority's sword is the institutional form of the Genesis 9:6 principle.

5.note: The Proverbs principle that 'through love and faithfulness sin is atoned for; through the fear of the LORD evil is avoided' — Paul's call to submit 'not only because of possible punishment but also as a matter of conscience' (dia tēn syneidēsin) grounds civic obedience in the same fear-of-the-LORD category that the Wisdom tradition uses for ethical formation.

Obey the king's command because you took an oath before God — the wisdom tradition that grounds civil submission in the God-commanded oath is the OT background for Paul's 'it is necessary to submit to the authorities, not only because of possible punishment but also as a matter of conscience'; submission has a conscience-dimension, not merely a fear-of-punishment dimension.

6.note: The Nehemiah community's burden of 'borrowing money to pay the king's tax' — taxes as a lived covenant reality within the Persian imperial framework that God's people navigated — provides historical concreteness for Paul's instruction. Payment of taxes to imperial authority was already a live ethical question for Second Temple Judaism under Gentile sovereignty.

The Mosaic half-shekel tax for the maintenance of the tabernacle — assessed on every man as a 'ransom for his life' — establishes the OT precedent that those who benefit from divinely-ordered community bear financial obligation to its maintenance. Paul's reasoning ('the authorities are God's servants, who give their full time to governing') mirrors this logic of financial accountability for ordered community life.

The Persian king ordering tax revenues for the rebuilding of the temple — the OT practice of paying taxes to pagan authorities (affirmed by Ezra and the returnees paying tribute to Persia) is the background for Paul's 'this is also why you pay taxes, for the authorities are God's servants'; taxing legitimate authority is itself a divine ordering.

7.note: The Wisdom command 'do not withhold good from those to whom it is due, when it is in your power to act. Do not say to your neighbor, Come back tomorrow and I'll give it to you — when you already have it with you' — Paul's 'give to everyone what you owe them' extends this Proverbial fulfillment-of-obligation ethic from economic neighbors to governing authorities.

Micah's summary of covenant obligation includes 'to act justly' — which in its social-legal register covers the rendering of what is owed to each person according to their proper due. Paul's taxonomy of what is owed (taxes, revenue, respect, honor) is a specification of this covenant justice applied to the civic domain.

Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due — Paul's 'give to everyone what you owe them' applies the Proverbs principle of rendering what is due to specific civic obligations: taxes, revenue, respect, honor.

8.note: The command 'love your neighbor as yourself' (which Paul quotes directly in v.9) is the 'continuing debt' Paul identifies as the one debt that can never be discharged — because love by its nature does not complete a transaction but generates ongoing obligation. Leviticus 19 embeds neighbor-love within the broader covenant stipulations that structure Israel's communal life.

9.note: Paul recites the second-table Decalogue commandments (no adultery, no murder, no theft, no covetousness) as the specific content that love summarizes. The Decalogue in Exodus was the covenant document's stipulation section — Paul argues that the commandments' moral content is not replaced by love but absorbed and exceeded by it: love cannot violate any of them.

Paul closes his citation of the Decalogue commands with 'love your neighbor as yourself' from Leviticus 19:18 — the governing principle that he calls the 'brief summary' (anakephalaiōutai) of the other commandments. Leviticus 19:18 stands in the Holiness Code between specific neighbor-obligations and functions as their affective ground: if you love as you love yourself, no specific violation is possible.
Commandments summed up in love for neighbor

10.note: Paul's statement 'love is the fulfillment of the law' (plērōma nomou hē agapē) applies the Levitical love-command as the summative principle: to love as Leviticus 19:18 requires is already to have done what the law requires. The fulfillment is not abolition but the actualization of the law's own goal.

The Shema's command to 'love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength' — Paul's neighbor-love fulfills-the-law claim assumes the double love commandment Jesus also taught (Matthew 22:37-40): Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18 together constitute the law's telos, which is what Paul here identifies as love.

Good will come to those who are generous and lend freely, who conduct their affairs with justice — the wisdom tradition's portrait of the good person who does no harm to a neighbor is the OT background for Paul's summary 'love does no harm to a neighbor; therefore love is the fulfillment of the law.'

11.note: Isaiah's vision of awakening — 'your dead will live, LORD; their bodies will rise. Let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy' — Paul's 'the hour has already come for you to wake up from your slumber' reaches for this eschatological awakening register. The night of exile and judgment is ending; the resurrection-dawn is approaching.

Joel's prophecy that the day of the LORD is coming — 'the sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD' — but also that 'everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved' (Joel 2:32, cited by Paul in Romans 10:13). Paul's eschatological urgency in vv.11-14 operates within this Joeline Day-of-the-Lord horizon.

Maintain justice and do what is right, for my salvation is close at hand — Paul's 'the hour has already come for you to wake from your slumber; our salvation is nearer now than when we first believed' is the apostolic version of Isaiah's eschatological urgency; the nearness of salvation is the motive for present ethical intensity.

12.note: Isaiah's prophecy that 'the people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of deep darkness a light has dawned' — Paul's 'the night is nearly over; the day is almost here' draws on this Isaianic light/darkness contrast, situating the Roman community at the threshold of the great light that Isaiah foretold and that Paul identifies with the coming of Christ.

God commands 'Arise, shine, for your light has come, and the glory of the LORD rises upon you. See, darkness covers the earth and thick darkness is over the peoples, but the LORD rises upon you and his glory appears over you' — Paul's armor-of-light imagery participates in this Isaianic day-of-glory dawn that requires the covenant community to adopt the posture of the approaching light, not of the departing darkness.

Arise, shine, for your light has come, and the glory of the LORD rises upon you — Paul's 'the night is nearly over; the day is almost here; let us put on the armor of light' inhabits the Isaianic dawn-metaphor for the eschatological era of divine presence; the light-armor of the new day is the ethical posture appropriate to those living in the overlap of the ages.

13.note: Isaiah's call 'Depart, depart, go out from there! Touch no unclean thing! Come out from it and be pure, you who carry the articles of the LORD's house' — Paul's 'let us behave properly as in the daytime, not in carousing and drunkenness' echoes this Isaianic purity-call that marks the community of the LORD's return as set apart from the behaviors of the surrounding world.

Do not join those who drink too much wine or gorge themselves on meat, for drunkards and gluttons become poor — Paul's 'not in carousing and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and debauchery' draws on the wisdom tradition's consistent catalogue of behaviors that belong to the darkness of self-indulgence rather than the light of disciplined life.

14.note: Isaiah's vision of the one clothed with 'garments of salvation' and 'a robe of righteousness, as a bridegroom adorns his head like a priest, and as a bride adorns herself with her jewels' — Paul's 'clothe yourselves with the Lord Jesus Christ' places the eschatological-clothing metaphor in christological focus: Christ is himself the garment that constitutes the new-creation identity.

The high priest Joshua standing before the Angel of the LORD in filthy garments, then being reclothed in clean garments and a clean turban — God's priestly re-clothing as removal of iniquity — anticipates Paul's metaphor. To put on Christ is to receive the clean garments that the judgment-scene of Zech 3 enacts as God's verdict over the defiled.

1.note: Isaiah's portrait of the Shepherd who 'gently leads those that have young' and 'gathers the lambs in his arms' — the community's obligation to 'accept the one whose faith is weak' reflects the divine shepherding posture toward the vulnerable. The strong are to deal with the weak as God deals with the fragile: receiving them without requiring that they be strong before they qualify for community.

God indicts Israel's shepherds for not strengthening 'the weak or heal[ing] the sick or bind[ing] up the injured or bring[ing] back the strays' — Paul's command to welcome the weak in faith is the positive fulfillment of the shepherding obligation Ezekiel's oracle found catastrophically absent. The community must exercise the care God's own shepherds failed to provide.

God welcoming foreigners and eunuchs who join themselves to the LORD — Paul's 'accept the one whose faith is weak' applies the OT principle of welcoming the marginalized who maintain covenant devotion even if their practice differs from the dominant community norm; the weak-in-faith Roman believers are the new covenant analog of those the majority would exclude.

2.note: Daniel and his companions refusing the king's food and wine, sustained by vegetables and water while remaining healthier than those who ate the royal ration — the Jewish practice of restricting diet to avoid defilement that Paul's 'weak in faith' community members maintain is heir to precisely this tradition of dietary boundary-keeping as covenant fidelity in a Gentile environment.

The Mosaic food laws — the comprehensive system of clean and unclean animals that defined Israel's table as a bounded covenant space — form the background for the faith-weak believers' vegetarianism. Their refusal to eat anything (in order to avoid possibly unclean meat) is a maximally cautious application of Levitical food law in a context where purity cannot be verified.

3.note: The Holiness Code's command 'do not pervert justice; do not show partiality to the poor or favoritism to the great, but judge your neighbor fairly' — both the contempt of the strong and the condemnation of the weak violate this covenantal impartiality. Paul's symmetrical prohibition against contempt and judgment on either side applies Lev 19 to intra-community dietary disputes.

4.note: Eliphaz's rhetorical question 'Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can even a strong man be more pure than his Maker?' — the rhetorical form of Paul's 'who are you to judge someone else's servant?' is the same: both use a question to expose the absurdity of human beings setting themselves above the One who holds judgment. All human beings stand as servants before a single Master.

The declaration that 'the earth is the LORD's, and everything in it; the world, and all who live in it' — the servant belongs to the Master, not to the one asking the question. Paul's 'to their own master, servants stand or fall' presupposes this divine ownership: both the strong and the weak are God's servants, answerable to him alone.

A prudent servant will rule over a disgraceful son — Paul's 'who are you to judge someone else's servant? To their own master, servants stand or fall' applies the patron-client wisdom about the household authority structure; judging another person's servant is a presumption of authority that belongs to the master alone.

5.note: The Mosaic calendar of appointed feasts — sabbaths, Passover, Pentecost, the Day of Atonement, Tabernacles — constitutes the OT background for 'one person considers one day more sacred than another.' Jewish believers observing the Mosaic festival calendar would regard these days as distinctively holy; Paul's 'each of them should be fully convinced in their own mind' grants this a personal conscience-authority rather than a community-imposable rule.

Paul elsewhere notes that food, drink, and festival observances (including Sabbath) are shadows of the reality found in Christ — the dispute about days in Romans 14:5-6 stands in this same framework; one person considers one day more sacred than another on conscience grounds without this being either required or prohibited for all.

6.note: The tithe and feast laws that require eating before the LORD and giving thanks — the principle that eating and refraining both done 'to the Lord, for they give thanks to God' is already embedded in the Deuteronomic feast legislation, where eating was itself a theological act performed in God's presence with thanksgiving. Paul extends this eucharistic character of eating to cover all food, not just tithe-food.

Eat what you desire at the appointed place and rejoice before the LORD — the OT principle that eating before God (with thanksgiving) consecrates the meal is the background for Paul's 'whoever eats meat does so to the Lord, for they give thanks to God'; the thanksgiving that accompanies eating is the covenantal act that makes the meal an act of worship rather than mere consumption.

7.note: The Psalm's declaration 'know that the LORD is God. It is he who made us, and we are his; we are his people, the sheep of his pasture' — 'none of us lives for ourselves alone, and none of us dies for ourselves alone' follows from this Psalmic confession of belonging: those who are the LORD's property cannot be autonomous owners of their own lives or deaths.

God's assertion 'every living soul belongs to me, the father as well as the son — both alike belong to me' — Paul's argument that neither living nor dying belongs to the individual self rests on this Ezekielian claim of divine ownership over every human life, which is the precondition for all judgment and all mercy.

8.note: David's prayer 'into your hands I commit my spirit; deliver me, LORD, my faithful God' — the entrusting of life and death to God that is the classic OT gesture of covenant belonging. Paul's 'whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord' is the theological generalization of this Psalmic act: belonging to the Lord is the comprehensive category that frames both states.

The Psalm's declaration 'precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his faithful servants' — even death is within the domain of the LORD's covenant interest. Paul's 'if we die, we die for the Lord' draws on this Psalmic conviction that death does not place the believer outside God's economy but within it.

9.note: The Psalm declares 'our God is a God who saves; from the Sovereign LORD comes escape from death' — Christ's resurrection and lordship over both dead and living is the fulfillment of this Psalmic claim. The God of Ps 68 who 'saves from death' is identified as the Father whose Son died and rose precisely to exercise this authority.

Daniel's vision that 'multitudes who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake: some to everlasting life, others to shame and everlasting contempt' — Christ becoming 'Lord of both the dead and the living' presupposes this resurrection-from-death reality that Daniel already saw.
